metadata.summary: Dynamic IP banning layer for Ubuntu 24.04 servers: SSH jail, nginx jails, custom filters, recidive escalation, Cloudflare whitelisting.

content.title: fail2ban Setup for Ubuntu 24.04
content.summary: Dynamic IP banning layer for Ubuntu 24.04 servers using nftables backend and systemd journal log source: SSH jail (maxretry 3), nginx jails (botsearch, limit-req, http-auth), custom application filters, recidive jail for week-long all-ports bans on repeat offenders, and Cloudflare IP whitelisting. Configuration exclusively via drop-in files in jail.d/ and filter.d/ — never edit jail.conf directly.

why.p: Even with SSH key-only auth and a firewall, servers face thousands of automated SSH brute-force attempts and web scanner bots daily. fail2ban monitors logs and temporarily bans IPs exhibiting malicious behavior, adding a dynamic defense layer at zero throughput cost. The recidive jail catches IPs that evade short bans by returning repeatedly, escalating to a one-week all-ports block.

list.item: After SSH hardening — fail2ban is the dynamic complement to static firewall rules
When adding any publicly exposed service (nginx, mail server) — add a jail for that service
When nginx logs show repeated scanner traffic for the same IP — verify filter match, then enable jail
Deploying behind Cloudflare — configure real-IP extraction so the actual attacker IP is banned
Servers behind a private VPN where SSH is not reachable from the public internet
Kubernetes pods / Docker containers logging to Docker log driver (not a file fail2ban can tail)
As a substitute for SSH key-only auth — it is a complement, not a replacement
Cloudflare Workers or serverless — no server-level log to monitor

section.name: fail2ban-architecture
section.p: Log files or systemd journal → fail2ban-server → filters match patterns → jails count matches → actions ban/unban via nftables.

rule.statement: Use backend=systemd for services that log to journald (sshd on Ubuntu 24.04). Use backend=auto for services with explicit log files (nginx). Never set logpath when using backend=systemd — it is silently ignored.
rule.rationale: The systemd backend reads the journal directly; there is no log file path. Including logpath with backend=systemd triggers a warning and may prevent the jail from starting correctly.

rule.statement: Never edit jail.conf or fail2ban.conf. Create all customizations in jail.local (global defaults) and jail.d/*.conf (per-jail drop-ins). These survive package upgrades.
rule.rationale: jail.conf is overwritten on package upgrade. jail.local and jail.d/ files are preserved. This mirrors the sshd_config.d/ pattern.

list.item: /etc/fail2ban/jail.local — global defaults (bantime, findtime, maxretry, ignoreip, backend, banaction)
/etc/fail2ban/jail.d/*.conf — per-jail drop-ins
/etc/fail2ban/filter.d/*.conf — filter regex patterns
/etc/fail2ban/action.d/*.conf — ban/unban actions

rule.statement: Set banaction=nftables and banaction_allports=nftables[type=allports] in [DEFAULT]. Ubuntu 24.04 uses nftables as the default firewall backend; mixing iptables and nftables rules causes conflicts.
rule.rationale: fail2ban defaults to iptables on older systems. Ubuntu 24.04 uses nftables via UFW. Setting the nftables banaction explicitly ensures fail2ban's bans integrate correctly with the existing ruleset.

list.item: sudo fail2ban-client status — show all jails and summary
sudo fail2ban-client status sshd — detailed jail status with banned IPs
sudo fail2ban-client set sshd unbanip 1.2.3.4 — unban a specific IP
sudo fail2ban-client reload — reload config without clearing ban state (use instead of restart)
sudo fail2ban-regex /var/log/nginx/access.log /etc/fail2ban/filter.d/nginx-botsearch.conf — test filter
sudo nft list ruleset | grep -A5 "f2b" — inspect active nftables ban rules

rule.statement: Use fail2ban-client reload (not restart) after config changes. reload preserves existing ban state; restart clears all bans.
rule.rationale: Restarting fail2ban unbans all currently banned IPs, giving active attackers an immediate window to reconnect.

section.name: ignoreip-whitelist

rule.statement: Always whitelist localhost, Docker networks (172.16.0.0/12, 10.0.0.0/8), your static IP, VPN subnet, and all Cloudflare IP ranges BEFORE enabling any jail. A misconfigured whitelist bans you immediately on first nginx scan.
rule.rationale: fail2ban bans based on log patterns without geographic awareness. Your own monitoring tools, Docker containers, and Cloudflare proxy IPs all generate traffic that matches scanner patterns.

section.reference: Global jail.local with ignoreip and nftables backend

rule.statement: Configure SSH jail with maxretry=3, findtime=600, bantime=3600. Use filter=sshd[mode=aggressive] to catch invalid user attempts in addition to failed passwords.
rule.rationale: 3 failures in 10 minutes is aggressive enough to block brute-force (which tries hundreds per minute) while allowing legitimate multi-key authentication failures without permanent lockout.

list.item: nginx-http-auth — catches 401 errors from nginx basic auth (error.log)
nginx-botsearch — catches 404 probes for vulnerability paths (access.log), maxretry=10
nginx-limit-req — catches nginx rate limit violations (error.log)
nginx-bad-request — catches malformed HTTP requests, 400 responses (access.log)

rule.statement: Configure the recidive jail: if an IP is banned 3 times in 24 hours, ban it for 1 week on ALL ports using banaction=nftables[type=allports]. recidive requires /var/log/fail2ban.log to exist — logtarget must not be set to journal-only.
rule.rationale: Short bans (1h) do not deter persistent scanners. Recidive escalates to a week-long all-ports block for IPs that return after being banned, effectively removing persistent attackers.

rule.statement: Enable bantime.increment=true in [DEFAULT] with bantime.factor=2 and bantime.maxtime=604800 to automatically double ban time for repeat offenders within the same jail, up to 1 week maximum.
rule.rationale: Linear ban times do not deter automated scanners. Exponential escalation per IP makes repeated attempts progressively more costly without manual intervention.

rule.statement: Place custom filters in /etc/fail2ban/filter.d/<name>.conf with a [Definition] section containing failregex patterns. Use <HOST> as the placeholder for the IP address. Test every filter with fail2ban-regex before enabling the jail.
rule.rationale: An unmatched filter silently produces zero bans — there is no error. fail2ban-regex validates the pattern against real logs before deployment, showing match count and sample matched lines.

example.title: Custom filter for application authentication
example.note: datepattern may need to be set if the log format uses non-standard timestamp formatting.

rule.statement: Run fail2ban-regex against the actual log file before enabling any jail. Check that the match count is non-zero and that the IP capture group works (shows actual IPs, not empty). A zero exit code does not mean matches were found — check stdout.
rule.rationale: fail2ban-regex returns exit 0 even if no matches are found. Agents and operators must read the match count line explicitly.

example.title: Testing a filter against real logs
example.code: sudo fail2ban-regex /var/log/nginx/access.log /etc/fail2ban/filter.d/webapp-auth.conf # Check output for: # Failregex: N total (must be > 0) # Sample matched: 185.x.x.x -... "POST /api/auth/login..." 401

rule.statement: When nginx is behind Cloudflare, configure nginx real_ip module with Cloudflare's IP ranges and real_ip_header CF-Connecting-IP so nginx logs the actual client IP. Without this, fail2ban bans Cloudflare proxy IPs, blocking all traffic through that proxy.
rule.rationale: Cloudflare forwards traffic from a small set of well-known IP ranges. If nginx logs the Cloudflare proxy IP instead of the real client IP, fail2ban will ban Cloudflare itself, disrupting all CDN-proxied traffic.

example.title: Real IP extraction for Cloudflare

antipattern.description: Hardcoding Cloudflare IP ranges in nginx or fail2ban ignoreip. Cloudflare's IP ranges change over time.
antipattern.reason: Fetch the live list with: curl https://www.cloudflare.com/ips-v4. Stale hardcoded ranges miss new Cloudflare IPs and create silent gaps.

template.purpose: Global jail.local with ignoreip, nftables backend, and default ban thresholds
SSH jail drop-in for jail.d/ with maxretry and findtime
nginx jails drop-in: http-auth, botsearch, limit-req, bad-request
Recidive jail for repeat-offender escalation with week-long all-ports ban
